exercises.title: SwiftData (models, queries, relationships, migration) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of SwiftData?
options: SwiftData modernizes persistence ergonomics, but schema and ownership design still matter.

A strong answer compares SwiftData with Core Data by ergonomics and platform maturity. SwiftData is attractive when you want modern Swift APIs, but you still need to plan for schema evolution and testability.
SwiftData is Apple's modern persistence layer that integrates model declaration, querying, and observation more directly with SwiftUI-style development.
Treating the persistence model as the entire domain model can couple business logic too tightly to storage details.

exercises.title: Combine (Publishers, Subscribers, operators — enough to understand legacy code) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Combine?
options: Combine models streams of values over time with transformation and cancellation built in.

Strong answers compare Combine with async or await: one is about streams over time, the other about sequential asynchronous code. That distinction helps you explain when a publisher pipeline is still the right tool.
Combine is Apple's reactive framework for values over time, built around publishers, subscribers, and operators that transform asynchronous streams.
Large publisher chains become unreadable when each step does too much and no one names the intermediate intent.

exercises.title: Keychain & Security Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Keychain & Security?
options: Keychain is for sensitive values that need stronger storage guarantees than general app data.

A strong answer talks about threat models, secure storage boundaries, and how biometric prompts or access control improve the user flow without becoming security theater. The point is controlled access, not just 'use Keychain'.

The Keychain stores sensitive credentials and secrets with stronger protection than ordinary app storage, but secure design still depends on what you store, how you access it, and when it should be available.
Putting long-lived secrets in plain user defaults or files is a serious storage design mistake.
exercises.explanation: The Keychain stores sensitive credentials and secrets with stronger protection than ordinary app storage, but secure design still depends on what you store, how you access it, and when it should be available.

Letting UIKit delegate patterns leak unchecked into the whole SwiftUI layer creates confusing mixed paradigms.

exercises.title: CI/CD (Xcode Cloud, Fastlane, GitHub Actions) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of CI/CD?
options: CI/CD should make releases both faster and safer through repeatable automation.
In interviews, explain CI/CD as staged automation: validate, build, sign, distribute, and monitor. That framing shows you understand both developer feedback loops and release operations.

exercises.title: REST API (HTTP methods, status codes, headers, authentication, pagination, versioning, error handling) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of REST API?
options: REST design is about using HTTP semantics to communicate intent and outcomes clearly.

A strong answer compares endpoint structure and transport semantics to product needs. For example, a feed API, a checkout API, and an admin API may all use HTTP, but the safety and consistency expectations differ.
A REST API is a resource-oriented way to expose server capabilities over HTTP, where methods, status codes, and representations communicate what happened and what the client can do next.
Treating REST as 'just JSON over HTTP' misses the importance of resource modeling and protocol semantics.
exercises.explanation: A REST API is a resource-oriented way to expose server capabilities over HTTP, where methods, status codes, and representations communicate what happened and what the client can do next.

exercises.title: WebSocket (handshake, frames, real-time use cases, Socket.IO) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of WebSocket?
options: WebSocket is valuable when low-latency bidirectional messaging is part of the product experience.

Interview answers are strongest when they mention the trade-off: WebSocket gives low-latency updates, but you take on connection management, backpressure, and distributed systems concerns that simple request-response APIs avoid.
WebSocket provides a persistent full-duplex connection when client and server need to exchange messages continuously without repeated HTTP request overhead.
Choosing WebSocket for occasional updates adds connection complexity when polling or server-sent events might be enough.
exercises.explanation: WebSocket provides a persistent full-duplex connection when client and server need to exchange messages continuously without repeated HTTP request overhead.

Choosing WebSocket for occasional updates adds connection complexity when polling or server-sent events might be enough.

exercises.title: GraphQL (queries, mutations, subscriptions, vs REST) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of GraphQL?
options: GraphQL trades endpoint flexibility for more schema and resolver discipline.

Strong answers compare the API styles in context. GraphQL is especially attractive when multiple clients need differently shaped views of related data, but it can be unnecessary if a small, stable REST surface already fits the workload well.
GraphQL lets clients ask for precisely shaped data through queries, mutations, and subscriptions, which can reduce over-fetching but shifts complexity into the schema and resolver layer.
GraphQL does not automatically solve backend design problems; poor resolver structure can make performance worse than a simple REST API.
exercises.explanation: GraphQL lets clients ask for precisely shaped data through queries, mutations, and subscriptions, which can reduce over-fetching but shifts complexity into the schema and resolver layer.

exercises.title: Networking Fundamentals (HTTP/HTTPS, TCP/UDP, DNS, TLS/SSL, certificate pinning) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Networking Fundamentals?
options: Network communication depends on multiple layers that solve naming, transport, trust, and application semantics separately.

Strong answers connect the layers. For example, an HTTPS request usually relies on DNS lookup, a TCP connection, a TLS handshake, and then the HTTP exchange. Understanding that chain helps debug latency, trust, and connection issues more intelligently.
Networking fundamentals explain how machines find each other, establish trust, and move data across unreliable networks with predictable rules.
Treating all network failures as 'the API is down' ignores where problems can happen in the stack.
exercises.explanation: Networking fundamentals explain how machines find each other, establish trust, and move data across unreliable networks with predictable rules.

exercises.title: Networking Fundamentals (HTTP/HTTPS, TCP/UDP, DNS, TLS/SSL, certificate pinning) Check 2

Treating all network failures as 'the API is down' ignores where problems can happen in the stack.
Adding security mechanisms like pinning without planning for rotation and failure handling can create self-inflicted outages.

exercises.title: Authentication & Authorization (OAuth 2.0, JWT, API keys, session tokens, biometric auth) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Authentication & Authorization?
options: Authentication and authorization solve different security questions and should be modeled separately.
A good interview answer compares the mechanism to the threat model. For example, mobile client auth, backend service auth, and third-party delegated login are related but not identical problems.
Authentication answers 'who are you', while authorization answers 'what are you allowed to do'. Mixing them up produces weak system design and security holes.
Storing long-lived credentials casually or treating client-side role checks as real authorization is a serious design mistake.
exercises.explanation: Authentication answers 'who are you', while authorization answers 'what are you allowed to do'. Mixing them up produces weak system design and security holes.

exercises.title: Git (branching strategies, merge vs rebase, cherry-pick, stash, conventional commits, gitflow vs trunk-based) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Git?
options: Git proficiency is really about understanding history and collaboration trade-offs.

Strong answers focus on collaboration outcomes: cleaner reviews, safer releases, easier rollbacks, and less confusion when conflicts happen. Conventional commits and small branches help because they make intent and release notes clearer.
Git is a distributed version-control system for tracking changes, collaborating safely, and keeping a clear history of why code evolved.
Using Git commands by habit without understanding the resulting history makes conflict recovery and collaboration much harder.
exercises.explanation: Git is a distributed version-control system for tracking changes, collaborating safely, and keeping a clear history of why code evolved.

exercises.title: CI/CD Concepts (pipelines, build/test/deploy stages, environments) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of CI/CD Concepts?
options: CI/CD is about reliable feedback and release automation, not just a specific vendor or YAML syntax.

A strong explanation frames a pipeline as stages with clear inputs and outputs: fetch code, install dependencies, run tests, build artifacts, deploy, verify, and potentially roll back. That shows operational thinking, not just tool familiarity.
CI/CD is the practice of automating validation and delivery so software moves through build, test, and release stages reliably instead of by manual repetition.
Automating deployment without trustworthy tests and rollback plans can turn mistakes into faster outages.
exercises.explanation: CI/CD is the practice of automating validation and delivery so software moves through build, test, and release stages reliably instead of by manual repetition.

exercises.title: CI/CD Concepts (pipelines, build/test/deploy stages, environments) Check 2

options: A good pipeline is fast enough to use constantly and strict enough to catch risky changes.
CI/CD is the practice of automating validation and delivery so software moves through build, test, and release stages reliably instead of by manual repetition.
Automating deployment without trustworthy tests and rollback plans can turn mistakes into faster outages.
Pipelines that are slow or flaky lose credibility, so developers stop relying on them for feedback.
exercises.explanation: A good pipeline is fast enough to use constantly and strict enough to catch risky changes.

options: Pick storage technology based on consistency, query shape, and scaling needs.

exercises.title: Caching Strategies (in-memory, disk, HTTP caching, ETags, cache invalidation) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Caching Strategies?
options: Caching is a trade-off between speed, load reduction, and freshness guarantees.

A strong answer uses examples such as avatar images, feature flags, or product listings. That makes it easier to explain time-to-live, stale-while-revalidate, and what happens when cached data conflicts with user-triggered updates.
Caching improves speed and reduces load by reusing previously fetched or computed data, but it only works well when freshness and invalidation are designed deliberately.
Adding a cache without a freshness strategy simply trades latency problems for correctness problems.
exercises.explanation: Caching improves speed and reduces load by reusing previously fetched or computed data, but it only works well when freshness and invalidation are designed deliberately.

exercises.title: System Design Basics (load balancing, CDN, microservices, message queues, database sharding) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of System Design Basics?
options: System design starts with requirements, traffic, and failure modes, not with architecture buzzwords.

A strong interview answer starts with requirements and bottlenecks, then introduces components only when they solve a specific need. That is much better than drawing every famous box from a distributed systems diagram.
System design basics are about decomposing a product into services, storage, and delivery paths that can scale, stay available, and remain understandable under real traffic.
Adding distributed-system components before identifying the actual bottleneck creates complexity without value.
exercises.explanation: System design basics are about decomposing a product into services, storage, and delivery paths that can scale, stay available, and remain understandable under real traffic.

exercises.title: Security (OWASP mobile top 10, HTTPS, data encryption, secure storage, code obfuscation) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Security?
options: Security is threat-model-driven risk reduction, not a bag of unrelated best practices.

Strong answers connect controls to threats and acknowledge trade-offs. Security decisions affect UX, operations, recoverability, and developer workflows, so they cannot be treated as purely technical add-ons.
Security is the discipline of reducing risk by protecting data, identities, and execution paths against realistic threats, not by adding isolated checklists after the product is built.
Relying on client-side checks or obfuscation while neglecting server-side authorization is a fundamental security mistake.
exercises.explanation: Security is the discipline of reducing risk by protecting data, identities, and execution paths against realistic threats, not by adding isolated checklists after the product is built.

exercises.title: Code Quality (code review best practices, linting, static analysis, tech debt) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Code Quality?
options: Code quality is about preserving the ability to change software safely and confidently.

Strong answers focus on maintainability under change: can new engineers understand the code, can risky changes be reviewed safely, and do the tools catch obvious regressions before humans need to argue about style?

exercises.title: Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) Check 1
exercises.question: Which option best captures the core mental model of Dependency Management?
options: Dependencies save time only when their long-term maintenance cost is understood and acceptable.

A strong answer covers both speed and caution: dependencies can accelerate delivery, but every package is also a supply-chain and maintenance commitment. The best teams evaluate need, maturity, update policy, and exit cost before adopting one.
Dependency management is the practice of choosing, versioning, updating, and auditing third-party packages so the codebase stays buildable, secure, and understandable.
Adding packages for trivial problems increases attack surface and maintenance cost without meaningful leverage.
exercises.explanation: Dependency management is the practice of choosing, versioning, updating, and auditing third-party packages so the codebase stays buildable, secure, and understandable.

interview_questions.title: Variables, Constants & Type System · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Variables, Constants & Type System to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Value declaration defines mutability, inference, and how explicit your API contracts are. Then connect it to production behavior: Explain when immutable bindings are the default choice and when an explicit type is clearer than inference. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Using mutable state by default makes data flow harder to reason about and easier to break during refactors.
follow_up_questions: What tradeoff would make you avoid Variables, Constants & Type System in this situation?
How would you test or validate the decision behind Variables, Constants & Type System?
Which boundary or dependency becomes easier to manage when Variables, Constants & Type System is modeled clearly?
common_mistakes: Using mutable state by default makes data flow harder to reason about and easier to break during refactors.
Listing tools or APIs without connecting them to ownership, tradeoffs, or user impact.
Describing the happy path only and ignoring cancellation, failure, or migration behavior.

interview_questions.title: Null Safety (sound null safety, ?, !, late, required) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Null Safety (sound null safety, ?, !, late, required) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Nullability makes absence explicit and forces safe access before a value is used. Then connect it to production behavior: Describe the safe unwrapping path, the sharp edges of force access, and why explicit optionality improves correctness. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Teams introduce crashes when they bypass null checks instead of designing the API around missing state.

interview_questions.title: Layout System (VStack, HStack, ZStack, LazyVStack, LazyHStack, Grid, ViewThatFits) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Layout System (VStack, HStack, ZStack, LazyVStack, LazyHStack, Grid, ViewThatFits) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Layout systems translate parent constraints into concrete sizes and positions for each child view. interview_questions.question: Explain State Management (@State, @Binding, @StateObject, @ObservedObject, @EnvironmentObject) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: State management decides where truth lives, who can mutate it, and which views rebuild when it changes. interview_questions.question: Explain Localization (String Catalogs, Localizable.xcstrings) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Internationalization prepares the product for multiple languages and regional formats before strings start multiplying. interview_questions.question: Explain GraphQL (queries, mutations, subscriptions, vs REST) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: GraphQL lets clients ask for shaped data explicitly, but the client still has to manage caching and schema evolution carefully. Then connect it to production behavior: Be ready to compare GraphQL with REST in terms of query shape, caching complexity, and backend ownership. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Overfetching can be reduced, but complex schemas and normalized caches still need deliberate design.

interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Database design balances query flexibility, consistency guarantees, and operational cost. Then connect it to production behavior: Explain why relational and document stores optimize for different access patterns and consistency models. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Choosing a database by hype instead of query shape creates expensive migrations later.
follow_up_questions: What tradeoff would make you avoid Databases (SQL vs NoSQL, normalization, indexing, transactions, ACID) in this situation?
How would you test or validate the decision behind Databases (SQL vs NoSQL, normalization, indexing, transactions, ACID)?
Which boundary or dependency becomes easier to manage when Databases (SQL vs NoSQL, normalization, indexing, transactions, ACID) is modeled clearly?

interview_questions.title: Caching Strategies (in-memory, disk, HTTP caching, ETags, cache invalidation) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Caching Strategies (in-memory, disk, HTTP caching, ETags, cache invalidation) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Caching trades freshness for speed, so the invalidation rule matters as much as the storage layer. Then connect it to production behavior: Interview answers should cover cache key design, staleness policy, and what happens when the cache lies. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Fast wrong data is still wrong, so invalidation and revalidation rules must be explicit.

interview_questions.title: System Design Basics (load balancing, CDN, microservices, message queues, database sharding) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain System Design Basics (load balancing, CDN, microservices, message queues, database sharding) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: System design is about choosing boundaries and bottlenecks intentionally before scale forces bad defaults on you. Then connect it to production behavior: Discuss load distribution, state ownership, failure modes, and the first scaling move you would make. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Design answers feel shallow when they jump to microservices before naming the real bottleneck or constraint.

interview_questions.title: Security (OWASP mobile top 10, HTTPS, data encryption, secure storage, code obfuscation) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Security (OWASP mobile top 10, HTTPS, data encryption, secure storage, code obfuscation) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Security is about reducing blast radius: protect secrets, validate trust boundaries, and assume hostile conditions. Then connect it to production behavior: Strong answers mention transport security, secure storage, least privilege, and how sensitive data is rotated or revoked. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Security work fails when secrets live in source control or when trust is delegated to the client alone.

interview_questions.question: Explain Internationalization & Localization to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Internationalization prepares the product for multiple languages and regional formats before strings start multiplying. interview_questions.title: Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Dependency management keeps builds reproducible while controlling upgrade risk and package sprawl. Then connect it to production behavior: Talk about version pinning, lock files, security review, and what belongs in the app versus in a shared package. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Unpinned dependencies create non-reproducible builds and surprise regressions.
